label: SES-080 PI-052 chat-UI design + slicing session. Claude Code conversation conducted at Doug's terminal 2026-05-25 evening, opened against work_ticket WT-047 (the PI-052 kickoff body at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-chat-ui-design-kickoff.md`). ARCHITECTURE working mode throughout, no implementation code written. Output is the canonical design document at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-chat-ui-design.md` (840 lines) plus this triple-artifact close-out + the Slice-A kickoff body WT-048 for the first implementation session. Records landed: 1 session (SES-080), 1 conversation (CONV-050), 0 commits, 1 work_ticket (WT-048 = Slice-A terminal-spike kickoff body, status ready), 11 decisions (DEC-252 production UI surface, DEC-253 tool surface scope, DEC-254 auth model, DEC-255 tool-call confirmation policy, DEC-256 persistence model, DEC-257 streaming, DEC-258 multi-conv UX, DEC-259 cost visibility, DEC-260 model selection, DEC-261 slicing plan, DEC-262 hybrid kickoff pattern), 0 new planning items (PI-053+ candidates noted in the design doc but not adopted), references threading the audit chain (11 decided_in + 2 references to DEC-245 + 3 references to existing decisions + 1 is_about + 1 supersedes-relationship for DEC-262 supersedes-prior pattern), 1 resolves edge (none — PI-052 stays Open until the chat UI ships), 1 addresses edge (PI-052). Note: kickoff anticipated SES-078 but SES-078 was consumed in flight by the parallel PI-002 build closure conversation — this session is SES-080 by the recent parallel-sandbox rebasing pattern. WT-047 is consumed by CONV-050.

session.title: PI-052 chat-UI design + slicing — architecture for the PySide6 chat tab on the Anthropic API with native tool definitions; eleven decisions settled across Q1–Q10 + tool surface + auth + model + slicing + methodology
session.conversation_reference: Claude Code session at Doug's terminal, 2026-05-25 evening, opened in /home/doug/Dropbox/Projects/ClevelandBusinessMentors with project context but operating against the crmbuilder repo for the v2 work. Conversation opened against work_ticket WT-047 (PI-052 chat-UI design kickoff body) and consumed it.
session.topics_covered: Read-first per kickoff Tier 1–5: v2 CLAUDE.md / get_current_status (v0.6 shipped) / DEC-244 (claude.ai connector bug) / DEC-245 (architectural pivot to chat UI on Anthropic API) / PI-052 / SES-077 close-out; inspection of `crmbuilder_v2.mcp_server.tools` (44 functions, FastMCP `@server.tool()` registration pattern, all wrap `httpx.AsyncClient` against the REST API at 127.0.0.1:8765), `crmbuilder_v2.api.main` (FastAPI surface), `crmbuilder_v2.ui.main_window` (sidebar + QStackedWidget + RefreshService pattern, sidebar groups by entity category), `crmbuilder_v2.ui.app` (QApplication factory with Fusion-style + light palette + bundled Inter/JetBrains Mono per design pass), `crmbuilder_v2.config.Settings` (pydantic-settings, CRMBUILDER_V2_* env prefix). Architecture design: PySide6 chat tab in a new 'AI' sidebar group, ChatPanel owning ChatController owning ChatWorker (QThread hosting private asyncio loop), anthropic.AsyncAnthropic + httpx.AsyncClient inside the worker, tool dispatcher reusing mcp_server.tools surface via a Slice-C refactor that extracts a shared `tool_definitions()` helper feeding both FastMCP registration and the chat dispatcher. Worked through all ten kickoff open questions Q1–Q10 plus the goal-list strategic choices (tool surface scope, auth, model selection, slicing). Eleven decisions settled: DEC-252 (PySide6 tab as production surface — resolves DEC-245 Phase 2 deferred), DEC-253 (all 44 tools registered, partitioned by read/write for mode toggle — no MVP subset), DEC-254 (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env primary + system keyring fallback, never settings.json), DEC-255 (auto-execute tool calls with inline disclosure, per-session 'ask before write' toggle), DEC-256 (persistence to JSON files under ~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats/, governance-entity elevation deferred to candidate PI), DEC-257 (streaming on, QThread + asyncio bridge no qasync dep), DEC-258 (claude.ai-web sidebar pattern, single active chat), DEC-259 (per-turn footer + per-session header for cost/tokens with three-tier cache-hit display), DEC-260 (Opus 4.7 default, Sonnet 4.6 + Haiku 4.5 selectable in header), DEC-261 (slicing plan A–D — A terminal spike ~80 LOC, B PySide6 MVP tab with one tool, C full surface + persistence + caching + system prompt drafted, D polish + exports + error matrix), DEC-262 (hybrid kickoff pattern with WT inline summary + file pointer + 'file is canonical' rule, established as the standard for non-trivial kickoff bodies — settles the methodology question raised at the end of the kickoff). Three open questions deferred: Q4 (tool-result visibility default — follows from DEC-255 + design pattern, no DEC), Q6 (system prompt design — outlined in design doc §13, full settlement at Slice C with its own DEC), Q7 (conversation export as governance close-out — surfaced as PI-053 candidate, not adopted in v1). Design doc covers component breakdown, data flow, prompt caching strategy (three tiers: system block + tools block + rolling conversation prefix), UI layout sketch with header / sidebar / transcript / input, slicing plan with explicit acceptance criteria per slice, error recovery matrix (8 failure modes with defined behaviors including 401 / 429 / context-window-exceeded / Stop / app-close / keyring-unavailable), future PI candidates (PI-053 governance-entity elevation, PI-054 remote chat surface, PI-055 tool surface expansion), and an Appendix A diff sketch for the mcp_server.tools refactor that lets both FastMCP and the chat dispatcher share one source of truth.
session.summary: PI-052 chat UI design + slicing complete. Eleven decisions settled across Q1–Q10 + tool surface + auth + model + slicing + methodology. PySide6 chat tab as production surface confirmed (resolves DEC-245 Phase 2 deferred). All 44 tools registered with read/write partition for mode toggle. JSON file persistence under user data dir (governance-entity elevation deferred to candidate PI). Streaming on with QThread + asyncio bridge. claude.ai-web sidebar pattern for multi-conversation. Per-turn + per-session token+cost visibility. Opus 4.7 default. Four-slice implementation plan A–D with explicit acceptance criteria. Hybrid kickoff pattern (WT summary + file pointer + file-is-canonical rule) established as methodology standard. Design document at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-chat-ui-design.md` is canonical. Slice-A terminal spike kickoff body authored as WT-048 for the first implementation session.
session.artifacts_produced: Design document `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-chat-ui-design.md` (840 lines, sixteen sections + Appendix A). This close-out payload `close-out-payloads/ses_080.json` and its apply prompt `prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-080.md`. Slice-A kickoff body `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-slice-a-terminal-spike-kickoff.md` (the new WT-048's `work_ticket_file_path`). Eleven new V2 governance decisions DEC-252..DEC-262 settling Q1–Q10 + tool surface + auth + model + slicing + methodology. One new work ticket WT-048 (Slice-A kickoff) status ready. Conversation CONV-050 with the standard workstream/session/work-ticket edges (belongs to WS-010, records SES-080, consumes WT-047). One addresses edge from CONV-050 to PI-052 (the chat UI is not resolved by this session — design only; PI-052 stays Open until the chat UI ships).
session.in_flight_at_end: PI-052 stays Open. Next concrete step: Slice A implementation session against WT-048 — a ~80 LOC terminal-based Python script that demonstrates the chat loop end-to-end with three read-only tools. The script lives at `crmbuilder-v2/scripts/chat_spike.py`. Once Slice A lands and is verified, Slice B (PySide6 chat tab MVP) is unblocked; Slice B's kickoff body becomes WT-049 then. WT-047 is consumed by this session. The design document at `pi-052-chat-ui-design.md` is canonical and consulted by every PI-052 implementation session. Q6 (system prompt design) settles at Slice C; its DEC will be issued in the Slice-C close-out conversation. Three future PI candidates are noted in the design doc §14 but not authored as PIs here — Doug surfaces them later if usage demands.

work_tickets.work_ticket_title: Kickoff: PI-052 Slice A — terminal proof-of-concept Python script for the Anthropic-API chat loop with native tool definitions (~80 LOC, three read tools, no streaming, no caching)
work_tickets.work_ticket_description: Implementation kickoff for PI-052 Slice A per the slicing plan in `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-chat-ui-design.md` §10. Scope: write a standalone Python script at `crmbuilder-v2/scripts/chat_spike.py` (~80 LOC) that demonstrates the Anthropic API chat loop end-to-end with three read-only tools (`get_current_status`, `get_current_charter`, `list_recent_sessions`) hand-defined inline (no refactor of `mcp_server.tools` in this slice — that's Slice C). Validates the architecture before any UI work lands. Full kickoff body at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-slice-a-terminal-spike-kickoff.md` (`work_ticket_file_path`). Reads on session open: this WT, the file_path kickoff body, the canonical design doc `pi-052-chat-ui-design.md` (especially §1 Architecture overview, §7 Tool execution loop, §10 Slice A acceptance, §12 Error recovery matrix). Anticipated session at close: SES-081. Decision settled at session end: confirmation that the loop architecture works; this resolves no DEC by itself but unblocks Slice B (WT-049) which lands the PySide6 chat tab MVP. PI-052 stays Open through Slices A–C; resolves on Slice D close. WT-048 consumed when its implementation conversation lands.

decisions.title: PySide6 chat tab as the production UI surface for the chat UI add-on (resolves DEC-245's Phase 2 deferred choice)
decisions.context: DEC-245 (architectural pivot to chat-UI-on-Anthropic-API) deliberately deferred the production UI surface choice to a Phase 1 spike, listing four candidates: (a) terminal-based UI with Rich/Textual, (b) PySide6 chat tab inside the existing CRMBuilder desktop app, (c) Streamlit/Gradio web prototype, (d) custom FastAPI + React/HTMX web app on the existing Cloudflare Tunnel. The PI-052 design session conducted today reviewed each option against Doug's actual workflow (administrator working on CBM + v2 governance, runs the desktop app daily, single-user, local-only is acceptable, wants the chat tightly integrated with the rest of the v2 surface). The CRMBuilder v2 PySide6 app already has a working sidebar + QStackedWidget panel pattern, design tokens, refresh service, server lifecycle management, and a StorageClient REST wrapper. A chat tab slots in naturally as a new sidebar entry in a new 'AI' group.
decisions.decision: The PI-052 chat UI is built as a new PySide6 panel inside the existing `crmbuilder-v2` desktop application, registered as sidebar entry `Chat` in a new sidebar group `AI`. Terminal-based UI is preserved as the Slice-A proof-of-concept spike (`scripts/chat_spike.py`) but is not the production surface. Streamlit/Gradio and custom web app are dropped from consideration.
decisions.rationale: PySide6 reuses the existing app's lifecycle, styling system, refresh service, and storage client. The chat tab inherits the v0.6 styling design pass (design tokens, fonts, palette, WCAG-AA contrast gate) for free. It surfaces alongside the panels Doug already uses to read governance data — switching between Decisions and Chat is a sidebar click, not an app switch. Local-only is fine for v1 (the chat UI is for Doug at his desk; remote access would require auth + transport work that DEC-244 just demonstrated is risky). Terminal UI is useful for the Slice-A spike (validates the architecture before any UI code) but doesn't deliver Doug's actual use case of conversational governance work integrated with the rest of the v2 surface.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Terminal Rich/Textual UI: rejected as production surface — Doug uses the desktop app daily; a separate terminal tool would be a context switch, and the visual affordances for tool-call disclosure / cost display / multi-conversation sidebar are clunkier in a TUI. Kept as Slice-A spike form. (b) Streamlit/Gradio: rejected — adds a Python web framework dep, runs in a browser (separate from the desktop app surface), would still require a separate process that the desktop app would have to spawn or rely on. Solves a problem (rapid UI prototyping) we don't have because PySide6 widgets compose fast enough. (c) Custom FastAPI + React/HTMX web app on the Cloudflare Tunnel: rejected as out-of-scope per kickoff non-goals — would re-introduce the remote-access surface that DEC-244 shelved. Plausible future PI if Doug ever wants chat-from-his-phone.
decisions.consequences: DEC-245's Phase 2 deferred-choice item is resolved. Slice B onward in the slicing plan (DEC-261) targets PySide6 specifically. The existing UI base (panels, sidebar, refresh service, styling tokens) is the reuse surface. No new web stack dependencies enter pyproject.toml — only `anthropic` (Anthropic SDK) and `keyring` (system keyring) join the existing dep set. Future remote-access surface is not foreclosed but is explicitly out of v1 scope and would be a separate PI.

decisions.title: Chat UI tool surface scope = all 44 read+write tools registered out of the gate, partitioned by name-prefix for the mode toggle
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff asked whether the chat tab exposes all 44 MCP tools as native Anthropic API tools out of the gate, or a curated MVP subset. The 44 tools already exist as a complete, tested surface in `crmbuilder_v2.mcp_server.tools` — they have been driving the stdio MCP server for months and wrap every entity in the v2 governance schema (charter, status, decisions, sessions, risks, planning items, topics, references, catalog).
decisions.decision: All 44 tools are registered with the chat UI's Anthropic API tools block out of the gate. No MVP subset. The tool dispatcher partitions tools by name-prefix at registration time into `read_tools` (26 — get_*, list_*, catalog_*, list_*_for_*) and `write_tools` (18 — replace_*, create_*, update_*, delete_*, add_*). The mode picker in the chat header uses the partition to filter what the API sees in any given turn: `Full` exposes all 44, `Read-only` exposes only the 26 reads, `Ask before write` exposes all 44 but the controller intercepts every write tool_use for a modal Allow/Deny/Edit prompt before dispatching.
decisions.rationale: Cherry-picking an MVP subset would (a) add a maintenance burden — which tools are in vs. out, when does a new tool earn the chat surface — without solving any actual problem, (b) miss out on Claude's ability to chain tools naturally (e.g., 'what's the supersedes chain from DEC-245' → `list_references_from` → `get_decision('DEC-226')` → both tools needed), (c) force a decision Doug doesn't need today since the cost of registering all 44 is negligible (tool definitions add tokens to the API call but prompt caching mitigates that — see DEC-259 caching strategy). The partition-by-prefix is a single line of code that gives us the read-only mode toggle and the ask-before-write toggle as a side benefit.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) MVP subset = read tools only for Slice B/C, all tools at Slice D: rejected because the read-only mode toggle gives Doug the same safety affordance without forcing the implementation to gate by slice. (b) MVP subset = read tools + decisions/sessions/planning_items writes for Slice C, rest at Slice D: rejected as arbitrary — once we have the write surface partition, we have all the writes. (c) Curated subset by usage frequency: rejected — we have no usage data; picking a subset would be guessing.
decisions.consequences: The chat dispatcher exposes the full 44-tool surface. Tool-schema generation happens once at panel startup; the schemas are stable across the session. The Slice-C refactor of `mcp_server.tools` (Appendix A in the design doc) extracts a shared `tool_definitions()` function that both FastMCP and the chat dispatcher consume, so the two surfaces never drift. As new tools are added to the v2 governance schema (e.g., dedicated tool wrappers for deposit_events, workstreams, work_tickets — currently accessible only via list_references) they auto-flow into the chat surface.

decisions.title: Anthropic API key sourcing = ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var primary, system keyring (via `keyring` library) fallback; never persisted to settings.json
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Goal #3 raised the question of where the Anthropic API key is read from. Candidates were env var, settings.json, system keyring, or a dotfile. Doug's existing CRMBuilder v2 config is built on pydantic-settings with the `CRMBUILDER_V2_*` env prefix; settings.json is not currently in the design (config is env-only). The key is a long-lived secret that must not land in any committed file.
decisions.decision: The chat UI's API key sourcing precedence at chat-panel first activation is: (1) `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable if set; (2) `keyring.get_password('crmbuilder-v2-chat', 'default')` via the cross-platform `keyring` library (Linux SecretService / macOS Keychain / Windows Credential Manager); (3) a modal configuration dialog presented to the user with a paste field, a 'Save to keyring' checkbox (default on), and Save/Cancel buttons. The key is never written to `settings.json`, never logged, never stored in any file in the repository. On 401 from Anthropic, the in-memory key is cleared and the configuration dialog reopens with an 'invalid key' warning; the keyring entry is left untouched so the user decides whether to overwrite.
decisions.rationale: Env var as primary mirrors the rest of the v2 config pattern (every other setting comes from `CRMBUILDER_V2_*` env). Keyring as fallback gives Doug a way to set the key once and forget it without exposing it in his shell rc files (which often end up in dotfile-syncing repos). settings.json is excluded because it's a project file that ships to the engagement DB exports; a secret there would be a leakage risk. The modal dialog is the bootstrap path for first use. Refusing to enable the chat tab without a key is a fail-loud behavior (the sidebar entry shows an empty state with a Configure button, not a silently-disabled feature).
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) settings.json with a warning comment: rejected — secrets in committable files are a foot-gun regardless of comments. (b) Dotfile at ~/.anthropic-api-key: rejected — non-standard, dotfile-syncing tooling would expose it. (c) System env only (no keyring): rejected — would require Doug to set the env var in his shell rc which is most likely synced via Dropbox. (d) Keyring only (no env): rejected — env var is the standard the Anthropic SDK itself reads by default and the convention for API key sourcing across the ecosystem. (e) Anthropic Console SSO token via the SDK's auto-auth: not in scope; Console SSO isn't designed for desktop app integration.
decisions.consequences: Adds `keyring` to `pyproject.toml`. On Linux Mint (Doug's primary surface), `keyring` uses SecretService which routes through gnome-keyring — already present on the system. Headless / minimal Linux environments may not have a keyring backend; the configuration dialog disables the 'Save to keyring' checkbox with a tooltip in that case, and the key is held in memory only for that app run. No `.env` file is read by the chat UI; the env var must be set in the user's shell or by a wrapper script that launches the app. The MCP stdio server is unaffected — it doesn't make Anthropic API calls (the model is on the other end of the stdio pipe). Future remote-access surface would need to revisit this (a web app couldn't use the desktop keyring), but that's out of scope per kickoff non-goals.

decisions.title: Tool-call confirmation policy = auto-execute with inline disclosure; per-session 'Ask before write' mode toggle for sensitive sessions; never per-call modal in Full mode
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Q1 asked whether the chat tab executes tool calls automatically (especially write-surface ones like create_decision / update_planning_item / delete_*) or prompts the user 'Claude wants to call X with these arguments — Allow / Deny / Edit'. Trade-off framed as deterministic safety + UX friction (modal per call) vs. flow + trust (auto). Doug is the sole operator; the v2 audit chain (deposit events, references, supersedes edges) is the load-bearing safety mechanism.
decisions.decision: Default policy in `Full` mode: tool calls auto-execute. Every tool call is rendered inline in the transcript as a collapsed disclosure widget showing the tool name + a truncated args summary (always visible); click-to-expand reveals the full JSON args. After execution, the tool result is rendered as a follow-on disclosure widget showing a one-line synthesized summary (e.g., `↩︎ created DEC-253`); click-to-expand reveals the full JSON result. The chat header has a Mode picker with three values: `Full` (default — auto-execute everything), `Read-only` (only the 26 read tools are exposed to the API; writes are not in the tools block at all so Claude can't even attempt them), `Ask before write` (all 44 tools exposed; the controller intercepts every `tool_use` block whose tool name is in the write partition and surfaces a modal Allow / Deny / Edit dialog with the args before dispatching). Mode is per-session; switching mode mid-conversation affects the next turn.
decisions.rationale: Modal-per-call would be friction-heavy in normal use — a typical chat turn might dispatch 3–5 tool calls in rapid succession, and prompting for each would defeat the conversational flow. Auto-execute with inline disclosure preserves the audit transparency (every write is visible in the transcript and in the storage system's audit chain via deposit events) without forcing per-call interaction. The Mode picker gives Doug an explicit safety affordance for sessions where he wants the friction (e.g., 'let Claude help me draft this DEC but don't actually create anything until I review'). Read-only mode is the strongest gate because writes are removed from the tools block entirely — Claude can't even claim to have called a write. Ask-before-write is the middle ground — useful when Doug wants Claude to do real work but reserves veto on each commitment.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Modal per write call always: rejected as friction-heavy for the common case. (b) Modal per call regardless of read/write: rejected — would block trivial read chains. (c) Auto-execute with no disclosure: rejected — opacity defeats the audit transparency that the v2 governance design depends on. (d) Tiered: confirm delete-* only, auto-execute create-*/update-*: rejected as inconsistent — the partition would be opaque and the rule would need explicit documentation. The mode toggle captures the same trade-off cleanly. (e) Confirmation toggle per-tool-name configurable: rejected as over-engineered — the Mode picker covers the same need at the right granularity.
decisions.consequences: The transcript widgets (`ToolUseItem`, `ToolResultItem`) implement the disclosure pattern. The controller honors the mode toggle: Full skips confirmation, Read-only filters the tools block before sending to the API, Ask before write intercepts write-prefix `tool_use` blocks and pauses the loop awaiting user input. Mode is persisted to the chat session JSON file, so it survives chat reload. Switching mode mid-conversation is permitted and takes effect on the next API call. No modal interrupts in `Full` mode — the chat experience matches claude.ai-web's auto-execute-tools UX.

decisions.title: Chat session persistence = JSON files under ~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats/ ; first-class governance-entity treatment deferred to a future candidate PI
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Q3 asked whether chat sessions are first-class governance entities (a new `chat_session` entity type, persisted to v2.db, exposed via REST + MCP) or ephemeral / file-based. The kickoff flagged this as a methodology question — not just an implementation choice — because making chat sessions governance entities would establish them as 'first-class governance conversation surface', parallel to the existing methodology session/conversation/work-ticket/close-out-payload pattern.
decisions.decision: Chat sessions are persisted as JSON files at `~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats/<chat_id>.json`. One file per chat, atomic write via tmp-and-rename, file format documented in the design doc §6. Not stored in `v2.db`. Not exposed via REST or MCP. Not modeled as a governance entity. The conversation sidebar reads the directory at panel open and on file-watch events. Elevation to first-class governance entity (with its own SQLAlchemy tables, REST surface, MCP tools, and the ability to close out a chat as a methodology session record) is surfaced as a candidate PI-053 in the design doc §14 but is not adopted for v1.
decisions.rationale: A chat in this UI is an ad-hoc exploratory Q&A surface — fundamentally different in kind from a methodology session, which has explicit edges to workstreams / work-tickets / decisions and lives inside the audit chain. Modeling chats as governance entities would require ~5 new SQLAlchemy tables (`chat_session`, `chat_message`, `chat_tool_call`, `chat_tool_result`, `chat_session_usage`), REST + envelope + access layer + MCP tool surfaces for each, schema versioning and migration paths, and a confusing namespace overlap with the existing `session` entity. None of that is needed to deliver Doug a working chat UI. JSON files give us: persistence across app restarts, listability for the conversation sidebar, exportability via standard file tools, restorability if needed, and zero schema impact. If Doug later wants chat-sessions-as-governance (e.g., a chat that produces decisions should close out as a methodology session with explicit edges), that's a tractable future PI with full insight into actual usage patterns from v1.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) In-memory only, no persistence: rejected — Doug will want to resume conversations and audit what was said. (b) New SQLite table in v2.db without REST/MCP exposure: rejected — half-measure, gains DB transactionality but not the audit-chain benefit, costs schema work. (c) Full first-class governance entity now: rejected for v1 — over-scoped for the value delivered; better authored as a follow-on PI with v1 usage data informing the schema. (d) SQLite database file at ~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats.db (single file with multiple chats): rejected — adds a non-v2 SQLite surface and migration burden for no v1 benefit over per-chat JSON files; single-file-per-chat is simpler to inspect, back up, and version-control if Doug ever wants to.
decisions.consequences: The chat panel reads/writes `~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats/<chat_id>.json` directly. No `v2.db` schema changes. Conversation files are not exported via the `db-export/*.json` snapshot mechanism (they're per-user runtime data, not engagement state). Chats are not accessible via the REST API or MCP tools — Claude in another chat session can't see a prior chat unless Doug explicitly references identifiers from it. The deposit-event audit chain is unaffected; chat writes that go through v2 governance tools land in deposit_events as normal (the chat surface is just one more way to issue tool calls, indistinguishable from MCP stdio at the deposit-event level). Future PI-053 candidate is recorded in the design doc but not authored as a PI; Doug surfaces it later if v1 usage warrants.

decisions.title: Streaming on by default for the chat UI; threading model = QThread hosting a private asyncio event loop, no qasync dependency
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Q9 asked whether streaming responses should be on for v1 or deferred. PySide6's event loop integrates with asyncio via the `qasync` library (or similar bridges). Streaming gives 'type-as-you-go' UX but adds threading complexity. The Anthropic SDK supports streaming natively via `messages.stream(...)` returning an async iterator of events.
decisions.decision: Streaming is on by default in the chat UI. The threading model is: a `ChatWorker(QThread)` instance owns a private asyncio event loop started inside its `run()` method via `asyncio.run(self._loop_main())`. The worker holds the `anthropic.AsyncAnthropic` client and the `httpx.AsyncClient` for tool dispatch. The main (Qt) thread calls into the worker via `QMetaObject.invokeMethod` for slot invocations (`start_turn`, `stop`). The worker fans out streamed events back to the main thread via Qt signals (`streaming_delta`, `tool_call_started`, `tool_call_completed`, `usage_updated`, `loop_finished`). Signals are thread-safe in Qt's signal/slot mechanism. No `qasync` dependency.
decisions.rationale: Streaming materially improves the chat UX — assistants typing-as-you-go feels responsive; blocking until the full message arrives feels laggy on long responses. The Anthropic SDK's streaming API is well-designed and exposes the event types we need. For a single worker thread hosting a single asyncio loop with no other asyncio integration points, vanilla `QThread.run() { asyncio.run(...) }` is sufficient — `qasync` would add a dependency and additional integration complexity without delivering value for this specific pattern. Tool execution is `await`-able inside the worker (httpx.AsyncClient is async-native) so the chat loop and the tool dispatch share one event loop cleanly.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Blocking (non-streaming) for v1, add streaming in v2: rejected — UX degradation isn't worth the implementation saving; the streaming pattern is well-supported by the SDK. (b) Streaming with `qasync` event-loop integration: rejected as over-engineered for one worker thread; the QThread + asyncio.run pattern is mainstream PySide6 + asyncio integration. (c) Streaming in a separate process with IPC: rejected — process management adds significant complexity for no benefit when the worker is single-threaded. (d) Streaming in a thread pool executor: rejected — Anthropic SDK's streaming is async-native, ThreadPoolExecutor would force blocking adapters.
decisions.consequences: Adds `anthropic` to `pyproject.toml` (the SDK). The chat worker thread is the only asyncio loop in the desktop app; the rest of the app (storage client, refresh service, server lifecycle) is QThread + blocking httpx. The Stop button cancels via an `asyncio.Event` checked at every await point; partial transcript content is preserved on cancel. The worker shutdown on app close emits `loop_finished('app_closing')` after the current chunk completes and persists the session file one final time. Stream-event handling lives in `_dispatch_stream_event` inside the worker; tool args are accumulated across `input_json_delta` events and emitted once at `content_block_stop` (not per-chunk — args mid-build are not useful to render).

decisions.title: Multi-conversation UX = claude.ai-web sidebar pattern; one active chat at a time; no tabs
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Q8 asked whether multiple chat sessions can run simultaneously (tabs within the chat tab) or whether the UX is single-chat-at-a-time with history in a sidebar like claude.ai-web. The choice affects UI complexity, conversation-persistence design, and the threading model (multi-conversation tabs would require multiple worker threads or a worker pool).
decisions.decision: The chat panel uses the claude.ai-web sidebar pattern: a 240px left column listing prior chats ordered by `updated_at` descending, a right column showing the active chat. Click a sidebar entry to switch the active chat. The '+ New chat' button at the top of the sidebar creates a new session in memory (no file written until the first user turn). Only one chat is active at any time; the worker thread is shared across chats — switching the active chat resets the worker's `current_session` reference. If Doug needs two chats in parallel, he opens two instances of the desktop app (the storage system and the per-user chat directory are multi-process-safe). Right-click on a sidebar entry: rename, delete, export-as-markdown.
decisions.rationale: Single-active-chat matches Doug's existing mental model from claude.ai-web. Tabs would add UI complexity (tab strip, close-tab affordances, focus management) and a multi-worker architecture for what would be infrequent simultaneous use. The 'open two desktop app instances for two chats' fallback is sufficient — running two instances is already supported by the app and is cheap. The conversation sidebar makes prior chats first-class browseable without modal History dialogs. Auto-titling from the first user turn (`title = first_user_text[:60]`) matches claude.ai-web's UX. Day-boundary group headers ('Today', 'Yesterday', '3 days ago') are a small polish that materially improves browsing in a long list.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Tabs within the chat tab: rejected as over-engineered for the use case; the threading complexity (multiple workers or per-chat asyncio context) doesn't pay off. (b) Single chat with modal History dialog: rejected — modal browsing is a friction layer when prior chats are referenced often. (c) Master-pane + detail-pane like the rest of the v2 panels: rejected — the chat content is naturally vertical and full-height, not table-shaped; the existing panel pattern doesn't fit. (d) Pop-out chat into a separate window: rejected as out-of-scope for v1 (could be a future polish PI if Doug wants chat visible while working in another panel).
decisions.consequences: The chat panel is a 240px sidebar + main column layout. The conversation sidebar widget watches the `~/.crmbuilder-v2/chats/` directory for file events to refresh the list (file-watcher pattern matching the existing `RefreshService` design). Sidebar items use a custom `QListWidget` + delegate matching the v0.6 design tokens. Auto-titling is in-controller (no model call to summarize). Day-boundary headers are render-time decoration. Multi-process safety is inherited from the file-per-chat persistence model — atomic writes prevent corruption, and the file-watcher catches updates from other processes.

decisions.title: Cost and token visibility in the chat UI = per-turn footer + per-session running total in the header; three-tier cache-hit display
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Q5 raised cost transparency as a design choice: token usage + estimated cost displayed per turn / per session / hidden. Doug runs against the Anthropic API which is metered; cost visibility matters for everyday-use confidence — the chat is most useful when Doug doesn't have to wonder what a turn cost.
decisions.decision: Token and cost visibility is rendered at two scopes. (1) Per-turn footer attached to each assistant message: `↑input_tokens ↓output_tokens (cache cached_read_tokens) ~$cost_estimate` in small subtle text using `font.body.small` and `color.neutral.500`. (2) Per-session running total in the chat header: `↑total_input ↓total_output (cache total_cached) $total_session_cost` updated after every turn. Cache tokens are reported as a single 'cache' number (the sum of cache_read_tokens; cache_creation_tokens are billed once and are visible only on the first cached turn). Cost estimate uses a small client-side pricing table per model (Opus 4.7 / Sonnet 4.6 / Haiku 4.5) covering input / output / cache_read / cache_creation rates; the table is hand-maintained and updated when Anthropic changes pricing.
decisions.rationale: Per-turn footer gives immediate feedback on what just happened ('that tool-heavy turn cost $0.012'). Per-session header gives the running total ('this session has cost $0.094 across 12 turns'). Both update from the `MessageStopEvent.usage` data the Anthropic SDK returns at the end of each stream. The three-tier cache display (input / output / cache) makes the prompt-caching savings visible — Doug can see that 4.2k of 12.4k input tokens came from cache and understand why the cost is lower than 'tokens × input-rate' math would suggest. Hiding costs would force Doug to check the Anthropic Console for billing visibility — that friction is exactly what we're avoiding.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Hidden by default with a toggle: rejected — cost visibility is the kind of thing that's only useful when habitual; toggling it on adds friction. (b) Per-session only (no per-turn): rejected — per-turn feedback is the natural learning loop (Doug learns which kinds of questions are expensive). (c) Estimated cost only (no token counts): rejected — token counts matter for context-window awareness and cache-hit understanding. (d) Anthropic Console link with no in-app display: rejected — moves the visibility out of the conversational flow entirely. (e) Per-tool-call cost breakdown: rejected as too granular; token usage isn't reported per tool call by the API, only per message.
decisions.consequences: The header widget includes a `UsageDisplay` sub-component that updates on every `usage_updated` signal from the worker. The transcript items include a usage footer slot rendered on assistant message completion. The pricing table lives at `crmbuilder_v2/ui/chat/pricing.py` as a frozen dataclass — updated by hand when Anthropic announces price changes. Per-session totals are stored in `ChatSession.usage` (the `UsageRollup` dataclass) and persisted to the chat JSON file, so reloading a session shows the historical totals. The cost estimate is just an estimate — the Anthropic Console is the source of truth for billing.

decisions.title: Model selection in the chat UI = Opus 4.7 default; Sonnet 4.6 and Haiku 4.5 selectable in the header model picker
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Goal #7 raised model selection as a v1 design choice. Anthropic's current model family offers Opus 4.7 (highest capability, slower, costliest), Sonnet 4.6 (balanced), Haiku 4.5 (fastest, cheapest). Doug's use case (administrator working on v2 governance with complex tool surfaces and the methodology context) is capability-heavy and not particularly latency-sensitive. But there are turns where a faster cheaper model is fine (simple list-and-summarize queries) and Doug might prefer to choose.
decisions.decision: The chat header has a Model picker (`QComboBox`) with three values: Opus 4.7 (default — `claude-opus-4-7`), Sonnet 4.6 (`claude-sonnet-4-6`), Haiku 4.5 (`claude-haiku-4-5-20251001`). The selected model takes effect on the next turn — in-flight turns finish on whatever model was selected when they started. The selection is stored on the `ChatSession` and persisted to the chat JSON file, so resuming a chat resumes on its prior model.
decisions.rationale: Opus 4.7 is the right default for Doug's tool-heavy governance work — capability matters, latency is fine, and prompt caching mitigates a meaningful chunk of the cost premium. The Sonnet 4.6 + Haiku 4.5 options give Doug an explicit cost / latency lever for turns where the cheapest model that can answer is preferred (e.g., 'what was the title of DEC-244?'). Per-session model means he can spin up a fast cheap chat for browsing and switch to Opus for the substantive design conversation without losing context. Including Haiku 4.5 even though it's the weakest of the three preserves the option without committing to it as a default.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Opus 4.7 only, no picker: rejected — gives Doug no lever to optimize cost on simple turns. (b) Sonnet 4.6 default: rejected — Sonnet is competent but Doug's use case is unusually capability-heavy and Opus's superior tool-call accuracy + reasoning depth is worth the cost on most turns. (c) Picker with all current Anthropic models including older Opus 4.6 / Sonnet 4.5 variants: rejected as clutter — the latest three are sufficient. (d) Auto-select per turn based on prompt complexity heuristics: rejected as over-engineered — Doug knows the question, Doug should pick.
decisions.consequences: The `ChatSession` dataclass has a `model: str` field defaulting to `claude-opus-4-7`. The header model picker emits a signal that updates the session field on change. The worker reads `session.model` at the start of each turn. The pricing table (per DEC-259) must cover all three models with their actual current rates. The model picker is part of the per-chat metadata, not a global setting — Doug can have one chat on Opus and another on Sonnet simultaneously (across two app instances per DEC-258) without one bleeding into the other.

decisions.title: PI-052 slicing plan = four slices A (terminal spike), B (PySide6 MVP tab), C (full tool surface + persistence + caching + system prompt), D (polish + exports + error matrix)
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff Goal #9 asked for an explicit slicing plan: each slice end-to-end-runnable, with the first slice being a proof-of-concept spike that validates the architecture before significant UI work lands. The kickoff offered a starting hypothesis (Slice A ~50 LOC standalone script, Slice B PySide6 tab with one tool, Slice C all tools + UX refinement + caching, Slice D conversation persistence if chosen) — the design session confirms or revises that hypothesis.
decisions.decision: PI-052 ships as four slices. Slice A — ~80 LOC standalone Python script at `crmbuilder-v2/scripts/chat_spike.py`, three read-only tools hand-defined inline, blocking I/O, no streaming, no caching, terminal-based. Validates the chat loop end-to-end. Acceptance: ask Claude 'what's the current status?', see correct tool call and synthesized response. ~1 hour implementation. Slice B — PySide6 chat tab MVP, one tool wired (`get_current_status`), streaming on, worker-thread + asyncio bridge functional, in-memory only no persistence, API key bootstrap dialog working, mode toggle and model picker present but read-only. Acceptance: launch app → click Chat → ask status question → see streamed response with tool-use disclosure → close and reopen → chat history empty. ~1 day. Slice C — refactor `mcp_server.tools` into shared `tool_definitions()` helper (Appendix A in design doc), all 44 tools wired, JSON file persistence, conversation sidebar functional, prompt caching at all three tiers (system block, tools block, rolling conversation prefix), system prompt drafted (Q6 settles here with its own DEC-NNN), cost+token display wired, mode picker functional. Acceptance: multi-turn conversation produces governance writes that land in storage; chat persisted across restarts; cache-read tokens visible. ~2 days. Slice D — multi-conversation operations (rename, delete, export-as-markdown), error recovery matrix wired (the eight failure modes from design doc §12), token-usage display refined, sidebar staleness integration with the existing `RefreshService`, unit + end-to-end test coverage. Acceptance: WCAG-AA palette regression green; all error cases recoverable without app restart. ~1.5 days.
decisions.rationale: The four-slice partition fits the kickoff's slicing-plan template and gives each implementation session a contained scope. Slice A is the architecture-validation spike per the kickoff's risk-management framing — proves the Anthropic SDK + tool-loop pattern works before any UI code lands. Slice B is the smallest end-to-end PySide6 increment that exercises the threading + streaming pattern. Slice C is the substantive build that delivers the actual usable chat surface. Slice D is the polish layer. Each slice is independently reviewable and commit-able. The shared `tool_definitions()` refactor lands in Slice C rather than Slice B because Slice B only needs one tool (no value yet from the helper) and the refactor benefits from having Slice B's pattern locked in before generalizing.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Three slices (skip Slice A): rejected — the spike is cheap and de-risks the architecture; skipping it would push validation into Slice B where mistakes are more expensive. (b) Five slices with persistence and full-tool-surface separated: rejected — over-subdivision adds session overhead without proportional benefit. (c) Slice B with all 44 tools: rejected — the refactor adds risk for the first PySide6 increment; one tool is enough to exercise the threading pattern. (d) Slice C with the refactor as a separate slice: rejected — the refactor is mechanical and shipping it together with the tool surface that depends on it is cleaner.
decisions.consequences: Slice A becomes WT-048 (authored in this session at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-slice-a-terminal-spike-kickoff.md`). Slices B / C / D become WT-049 / WT-050 / WT-051 authored at their slice's design-session-equivalent moments — the design doc and any new learnings from the prior slice inform each next kickoff. PI-052 stays Open through Slices A, B, C; resolves on Slice D close. The implementation conversation for each slice is a normal Claude Code build-execution session.

decisions.title: Hybrid kickoff pattern (WT inline summary + work_ticket_file_path pointer + 'file is canonical' rule) established as the standard for non-trivial kickoff bodies
decisions.context: The PI-052 kickoff body itself raised this methodology question explicitly at its end: the kickoff was authored as a markdown file at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-052-chat-ui-design-kickoff.md`, with WT-047 carrying a 60-line summary inline plus a `work_ticket_file_path` pointer to the file. This mixed pattern differs from WT-046's pattern (the v0.7 dogfooding precedent) which carried the full body inline with no file. Doug should decide whether file-kickoff or WT-inline-kickoff is the going-forward standard. The design session is the natural moment to settle this since the design session is itself the proof of how the pattern reads in practice.
decisions.decision: The hybrid pattern (WT summary inline + `work_ticket_file_path` pointer + the file is canonical when summary and body drift) is the standard going forward for non-trivial kickoff bodies. WT-046's inline-only pattern remains valid for short kickoffs (e.g., a single-paragraph WT body for a one-day implementation slice) but the hybrid pattern is the convention for any kickoff body longer than ~500 words. The 'file is canonical' rule is encoded as a one-line note at the top of every hybrid-pattern kickoff file (matching the language at the top of `pi-052-chat-ui-design-kickoff.md`). The Slice-A kickoff authored in this same session (WT-048 at `pi-052-slice-a-terminal-spike-kickoff.md`) follows the same hybrid pattern.
decisions.rationale: The full body lives in a markdown file because long bodies are easier to author, browse, edit, and diff via standard text tooling than via the JSON-blob `work_ticket_description` field. The inline summary in the WT lives in the V2 governance DB and gives the discovery query (`what work_tickets address PI-NNN?`) a useful read-out without a file-fetch. The 'file is canonical' rule resolves the drift risk that the kickoff explicitly flagged — if the inline summary and the file body disagree, the file wins, because the file is the artifact authors edit directly. For short kickoffs the file is overhead — keep WT-046's inline-only pattern available for those.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Always inline (WT-046 pattern): rejected — long bodies in JSON fields are awkward to edit and review; markdown files are the right authoring surface for substantive content. (b) Always file (no inline summary): rejected — the discovery query loses its self-contained read-out and would always require a file-fetch to know what a WT is about. (c) File-only with a one-line `work_ticket_description`: rejected — same drawback as (b) plus the one-liner is less informative than a paragraph-summary. (d) Sync the file body into the WT description on every edit: rejected — adds a sync requirement (and a way for the two to drift) for no benefit.
decisions.consequences: Future kickoff WTs follow this pattern. The convention is encoded in the kickoff file itself (one-line note at the top of every hybrid-pattern kickoff). No tooling change required — the convention is human-facing. The MR pattern (WT-046) is retained as valid for short kickoffs. As the v2 governance schema's `work_ticket_kind` field grows new values (e.g., `slice_kickoff`, `design_kickoff`), this pattern is the default for all `kickoff_prompt` kinds. If a future entity-schema decision adds a `is_canonical_in_file: bool` flag on `work_ticket`, that flag formalizes the rule programmatically; for now the rule is documented and human-enforced.
